cards.front: Which parameter controls stability in the active RLC example?
What normalized denominator appears after substituting component values?
What is the pole formula for the active RLC example?
When are the poles real in this example?
cards.back: When the discriminant is non-negative, which gives |1-A| >= 2 sqrt(2).
The poles become purely imaginary, plus/minus j sqrt(2), so the system is marginally stable.

cards.front: What is the full stable region for the circuit?
cards.back: The circuit is stable for A < 1.

cards.front: What is the full unstable region for the circuit?
cards.back: The circuit is unstable for A > 1.

cards.front: Why does A > 1 make the complex-pole case unstable?
cards.back: Because the pole real part becomes (A-1)/2, which is positive, so the oscillatory envelope grows.

cards.front: Why is the active RLC example pedagogically useful?
cards.back: It shows that changing one coefficient parameter can move poles through the imaginary axis and change the stability class.

cards.front: What bigger lesson does this example motivate?
cards.back: It motivates Routh-Hurwitz: a method for testing stability from coefficients when direct root-solving becomes cumbersome.
